title: Galileo's Starry Night Discovery
story: Peering through his telescope on a chilly night, Galileo Galilei made a jaw-dropping discovery. He spotted three tiny dots of light dancing around Jupiter. These weren't stars, but moons! On January 7, 1610, Galileo became the first person to see Io, Europa, and Ganymede - three of Jupiter's largest moons. This amazing find helped prove that not everything in space orbits Earth, changing how we understand our solar system forever.
activity_question: What did Galileo discover around Jupiter?
follow_up_question: Can you name one of the moons Galileo discovered?

title: Sakigake: Japan's Comet-Chasing Space Pioneer
story: Zoom! On January 7, 1985, Japan launched its first interplanetary spacecraft. Named Sakigake, meaning 'pioneer,' this plucky probe blasted off to study the famous Halley's comet. This mission marked a giant leap for Japan's space program, proving they could reach far beyond Earth's orbit.

Sadako survived the atomic bomb as a toddler, but later faced a tough battle with illness. She bravely folded paper cranes, believing in an old legend that folding a thousand would grant her wish for health and peace.
activity_question: What did Sadako Sasaki fold, believing it would grant her wish?
bonus_fact: Sadako's story inspired the Children's Peace Monument in Hiroshima, where people leave paper cranes as symbols of peace.

title: The Man Who Tamed Time
story: Tick tock! Sandford Fleming was born in Scotland in 1827, on January 7. This clever engineer would grow up to change how we tell time around the world. Fleming created Universal Standard Time, helping everyone sync their clocks. Before his idea, different places used their own local time, which caused confusion for travelers and businesses.
activity_question: What did Sandford Fleming create?
follow_up_question: Why do you think having a standard time for the whole world is important?

activity_question: What was the name of the ship that anchored near the Chonos Archipelago?
follow_up_question: Who was the young naturalist aboard the HMS Beagle?

title: Fannie Farmer's Recipe Revolution
story: Kitchens across America sizzled with excitement on January 7, 1896. Fannie Farmer, a culinary pioneer, released her groundbreaking cookbook. 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' changed how people cooked forever. It introduced precise measurements, making recipes easy to follow. Suddenly, anyone could whip up delicious meals with confidence!
activity_question: What did Fannie Farmer's cookbook introduce?
bonus_fact: Fannie Farmer's book is still in print today, over a century later!

title: Hockey Heroes Triumph in Ottawa
story: Cheers erupted at Dey's Arena as the Ottawa Senators clinched victory in 1910. On January 7, they defeated Galt 3-1, completing a thrilling two-game sweep. This win secured the coveted Stanley Cup for the Senators, cementing their place in hockey history.
activity_question: Which team did the Ottawa Senators defeat?
bonus_fact: The Stanley Cup is the oldest trophy in North American professional sports.

title: Cracking the Code of Petroleum
story: Boom! In 1913, scientist William M. Burton made a groundbreaking discovery. On January 7, he patented a process to 'crack' petroleum. This wasn't about breaking dishes - it was a clever way to turn thick, heavy oil into lighter, more useful fuels. Burton's invention helped power cars, planes, and even your school bus!
activity_question: What did William M. Burton patent?
activity_choices: A new type of car engine
A way to power school buses
follow_up_question: Can you think of other things that might use petroleum-based fuels?

title: America's First Jet Takes Flight
story: Zoom! The skies were about to change forever. In 1944, the US Air Force revealed their secret weapon: the Bell P-59, America's first jet fighter plane. This speedy aircraft marked a huge leap in aviation technology. On January 7, pilots and engineers celebrated as the P-59 zoomed into the history books, ushering in a new era of faster-than-ever flight.
activity_question: What was the name of America's first jet fighter plane?
bonus_fact: Jet engines work by pushing out hot air very fast, creating thrust.
